entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: In the word — the real, functioning word, the meaning-bearer

entities.name: varṇa
entities.cite: phonemes na vidyante, are not found

entities.name: vākya
entities.cite: the sentence is the primary unit

axioms.name: avidyante
axioms.cite: phonemes na vidyante, are not found

axioms.name: niravayava
axioms.cite: Phonemes in turn have no avayavas, no parts

axioms.name: apraviveka
axioms.cite: words have no atyantam praviveka, no absolute separation, from the sentence

axioms.name: mukhya
axioms.cite: the sentence is the primary unit

denials.name: vidyante
denials.cite: the sphota that conveys meaning has no phonemic grain

denials.name: praviveka
denials.cite: the parts are nowhere to be found in the functioning whole

claims.name: avidyante
claims.cite: In the word the phonemes do not exist

claims.name: niravayava
claims.cite: in the phonemes there are no parts

claims.name: apraviveka
claims.cite: no absolute separation of the words

rejected_readings.label: phonemes_foundational
rejected_readings.rendering: Phonemes are the ultimate units of speech and are directly found in words.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that phonemes exist in words as foundational units, directly contradicting the verse's claim that phonemes are not found in the functioning word. The verse explicitly denies this via the doctrine of sphota—meaning is conveyed without phonemic grain.

rejected_readings.label: word_independence
rejected_readings.rendering: Words are absolutely distinct and independent units, wholly separate from sentences.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts absolute separation (praviveka) between words and sentences, which the verse explicitly rejects. The commentary establishes that words are apoddhara (extracts) from the sentence and have no absolute separation from it.

rejected_readings.label: infinite_partitivity
rejected_readings.rendering: Phonemes themselves can be endlessly subdivided into smaller and smaller parts.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that phonemes have parts (savayava), contradicting the verse's explicit claim that phonemes have no parts (niravayava). The commentary blocks infinite regress by denying sub-parts at the phonemic level.

notes: The contract encodes the anti-analysis thesis: phonemes and parts are not features of the functioning word but artifacts of analytical decomposition. The relation apraviveka (no absolute separation) establishes words as dependent on sentences, rejecting atomistic theories that treat words as independent units. Sanskrit technical terms used: avidyante (not found), niravayava (without parts), apraviveka (no absolute separation), apoddhara (extracts/abstractions).
